title: SREs To AI Agents: Prove Yourself Before You Touch Production
gist: A sponsored piece built on a 696-respondent survey (The Register x NeuBird AI, April 2026) finds 73% of enterprises are not using AIOps at all and trust, not ROI or security, is the dominant blocker (60%), while NeuBird AI field CTO Francois Martel argues the winning approach is specialized agents with built-in explainability and context engineering rather than bigger models, and predicts observability infrastructure will shift toward cheap open-source backends (Grafana, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch) once AI-driven investigation decouples insight from proprietary telemetry storage.

insights.claim: A survey of 696 IT operations experts conducted by The Register with NeuBird AI in April 2026 found 73% are not using AIOps at all, 19% are in pilot, and only 8% have it in production.
insights.speaker: The Next Platform
insights.so_what: Quantifies how early-stage the AIOps/autonomous-remediation market still is; investors should treat AIOps vendor revenue claims skeptically given single-digit production penetration industry-wide.
insights.quote: A survey of 696 experts that The Register ran with NeuBird AI in April 2026 found that 73 percent are not using AIOps at all, another 19 percent are in pilot, and only eight percent have it in production.

insights.claim: In the same survey, 60% of respondents cited lack of trust as the primary barrier to AIOps adoption, far outweighing ROI, security, and data quality concerns which each registered around 12-13%.
insights.speaker: The Next Platform
insights.so_what: Trust, not cost-justification or security, is the binding constraint on AIOps sales cycles, meaning vendors that can demonstrate auditable, explainable decision-making have a differentiated go-to-market advantage over pure-automation pitches.
insights.quote: Asked what is stopping them, 60 percent of respondents cited a lack of trust, by far the biggest issue, with concerns about ROI, security and data quality each registering at around 12 percent to 13 percent.

insights.claim: Agentic workload categories with an obvious human-in-the-loop and clear verification path, such as coding agents and content generation, have achieved real adoption, while operations automation lags because it acts on live production data with customer-facing consequences.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: Provides a general framework investors can apply across agentic AI verticals: adoption speed correlates with ease of human verification, so operations/infra automation, healthcare, and finance agents will lag coding/content agents by design, not by technology maturity alone.
insights.quote: There's a lot of interest, but not a lot of action... Operations is harder, because the work happens inside the running environment, on data the engineer hasn't seen yet, with consequences that show up in customer-facing systems.
insights.locator: Lots Of Interest, Very Little Deployment

insights.claim: Martel observed inside enterprises prior to joining NeuBird a backlog of 300 candidate AI fixes accumulate with a year-long delay before the first one actually shipped to production.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: Illustrates the real-world lag between AI pilot enthusiasm and production deployment in ops contexts, useful for calibrating revenue-recognition timelines for AIOps vendors selling into large enterprises.
insights.quote: He saw the same gap inside enterprises long before he joined NeuBird: a backlog of 300 candidate AI fixes and a flurry of early enthusiasm, followed by a year of slog before the first one shipped.
insights.locator: Lots Of Interest, Very Little Deployment

themes.theme: Trust and explainability as the product

insights.claim: NeuBird AI's design philosophy treats demonstrated learning and improvement over time as the primary trust-building mechanism for operations agents, rather than one-time accuracy benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: Signals that AIOps buyers should evaluate vendors on longitudinal track record and audit trails rather than point-in-time accuracy claims; a differentiator investors can use to separate credible AIOps players from hype-driven ones.
insights.quote: Working with AI is a trust-building exercise, and AI has to learn in order to gain trust... If you can show that you're learning and getting better, then you can gain trust.

insights.claim: NeuBird AI logs the full reasoning behind every agent decision via Langfuse and is SOC 2 Type II certified, read-only, and stores no customer data, embedding auditability and security into the architecture rather than adding it as a compliance afterthought.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: A read-only, no-data-retention architecture lowers the security/compliance barrier for enterprises granting an AI agent access to production telemetry, a likely prerequisite for any AIOps vendor to clear enterprise security review.
insights.quote: Internally, NeuBird AI captures every reasoning step via Langfuse... The platform is also SOC 2 Type II certified, read-only, and stores nothing, so trust is built into the architecture, not just the reasoning.

insights.claim: NeuBird AI found that dumping full reasoning detail on users backfired, with early versions described as a 'wall of text'; the fix was making system reasoning interrogable via chat rather than presented all at once.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: A concrete UX lesson for the AIOps/agent tooling category: raw transparency is not sufficient for adoption; interface design for interrogating an agent's reasoning is itself a product moat.
insights.quote: early versions of the system surfaced so much detail that users described it as a wall of text. The fix was to make the reasoning interrogable rather than dumped, so engineers can chat with the system's memory the way they'd query a more senior teammate.

insights.claim: 59% of survey respondents require near-perfect accuracy before adopting AIOps, and Martel argues this bar can only be met through better context engineering rather than larger models.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: A direct pushback against the industry narrative that scaling model size drives better agent performance; investors evaluating AI-ops or vertical-agent startups should weight context/data-engineering moats over raw model access, since model access is increasingly commoditized.
insights.quote: That bar is unforgiving, and Martel argues it can only be cleared with better context engineering, not bigger models. The key to accuracy is this sweet spot between just enough context so that you're not missing things, and then discoverability of context... Certainly not too much context.
insights.locator: Context Is What Makes The Answer Credible

insights.claim: NeuBird AI pre-builds dependency mapping across an enterprise's technology silos before an incident occurs, so that an investigation already knows where to look once triggered, addressing the problem that no single human has full visibility across microservice-fragmented estates.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: Pre-computed dependency graphs, not just faster querying at incident time, are positioned as the differentiator for AIOps root-cause accuracy — a technical moat investors can look for when evaluating competing AIOps platforms.
insights.quote: NeuBird handles this by doing the dependency mapping before the incident starts, so that when an investigation kicks off the system already knows where to look and how the pieces relate.
insights.locator: Context Is What Makes The Answer Credible

insights.claim: 62% of survey respondents prefer AI to assist rather than replace human operators, and NeuBird AI is rolling out automation gradually via Ansible's Model Context Protocol server, with only certain playbooks marked safe for autonomous production action while the rest require human approval.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: Establishes that full autonomous remediation in production ops is not yet the market's near-term ask; vendors and investors should price in a multi-year co-pilot phase before autonomous-agent revenue models (e.g., outcome-based pricing) become viable in this segment.
insights.quote: The clearest signal from the research, and the one Martel finds least surprising, is the preference for co-pilot models, with 62 percent wanting AI to assist rather than replace.

insights.claim: Just over half of survey respondents expect operational answers within five minutes and 75% want them within ten minutes, a response-time bar that legacy multi-team war-room incident processes cannot meet.
insights.speaker: The Next Platform
insights.so_what: Quantifies the SLA pressure driving AIOps demand; vendors that can credibly compress mean-time-to-triage below five to ten minutes have a clear, measurable value proposition to sell against.
insights.quote: just over half of survey respondents expect operational answers in under five minutes, and 75 percent want them inside ten, putting immense pressure on workflows that were never built for the cadence.
insights.locator: The Five Minute Clock And The Death Of The War Room

insights.claim: 52% of survey respondents would consider switching telemetry/observability tools if AI-driven insights worked across any back-end, and Martel predicts observability will become dominated by open-source, cost-effective storage/indexing technologies like Grafana, Elasticsearch, or OpenSearch.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: A direct threat thesis to incumbent proprietary observability vendors (e.g., Datadog, Splunk, New Relic): if an AI reasoning layer becomes back-end agnostic, telemetry storage commoditizes toward cheap open-source options and value shifts to the intelligence layer sitting above it — a thesis worth testing against incumbent observability vendor pricing power and retention.
insights.quote: the most provocative finding, for incumbent observability vendors at least, is that 52 percent of respondents would consider switching telemetry tools if AI-driven insights worked across any back-end... In the future observability will be dominated by open source, cost effective storage indexing technology like Grafana, Elasticsearch, or OpenSearch.
insights.locator: What IT Means For The Observability Bill

insights.claim: Martel argues that in an AI-driven observability future, the strategic value shifts from whoever holds the most telemetry data to whoever can investigate it most intelligently, i.e., a context engine layered above the cheapest available storage.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: Reframes competitive moats in the observability/AIOps category away from data-hoarding lock-in toward reasoning/context-engine quality, a thesis relevant to valuing observability incumbents versus AI-native challengers.
insights.quote: In that scenario the strategic asset shifts from whoever hoards the most telemetry to whoever can investigate it most intelligently, which means a context engine sitting above whatever storage layer is cheapest.
insights.locator: What IT Means For The Observability Bill

insights.claim: Martel predicts that AIOps platforms surviving the next two years will be those that demonstrate their reasoning and integrate into existing enterprise change-management processes rather than demanding organizations rebuild workflows around them.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: A concrete two-year survival framework for evaluating AIOps vendors: prioritize those with explainability and workflow-fit over those pitching rip-and-replace automation.
insights.quote: Martel's pitch is that the platforms surviving the next two years will be the ones that show their work and fit into the existing change-management apparatus rather than demanding a rewrite of it.

insights.claim: Martel argues enterprises facing flat operational budgets and growing production estates will struggle competitively if they do not adopt AIOps, framing non-adoption as a growing operational risk rather than a neutral wait-and-see choice.
insights.speaker: Francois Martel (NeuBird AI)
insights.so_what: A vendor-interested but directionally useful signal: budget-flat IT organizations facing headcount constraints are a structural demand driver for AIOps tooling, relevant to sizing the addressable market.
insights.quote: There are advantages you will gain in terms of keeping up with a growing production estate with flat operational budgets... If you don't adopt it, what are you going to do? You are going to struggle.
